cataloging_subject.label: The History of the Holy Grail
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is Henry Lovelich's 1420s Middle English verse translation and adaptation of the Old French Estoire del Saint Graal, represented by the unique, imperfect witness in Cambridge, Corpus Christi College, Parker Library, MS 80. The checked XML begins at the first surviving English line and ends with Lovelich's prayer for completion. The abstract work is distinguished from Furnivall's modern chapter arguments and notes, his Old French replacement and explanatory passages, the retained 1905 EETS series half-title, the separate Merlin text that follows in the manuscript, and all digital-representation layers.

catalog_summary.title: The History of the Holy Grail
catalog_summary.author: Henry Lovelich
catalog_summary.date: Translated in the 1420s; first attested c. 1450–1475; first verified publication 1861–1863
catalog_summary.region: London, England; surviving-witness dialect localized to Essex

assertion.notes: No title in the surviving manuscript is asserted as the preferred title.

titles.value: Seynt Graal, or The Sank Ryal

scope.description: Earlier edition title for Lovelich's poem

titles.value: For that power haven not we

scope.description: First surviving Middle English verse line; the original opening is physically lost

titles.value: And bringen this book to A Good Ende. / Now therto Iesu Crist grace me sende; / And that an Ende there-Offen myhte be, / Now, goode lord, graunt me for Charyte.

assertion.notes: The final four encoded verse lines close Lovelich's self-identifying prayer.

agents.name: Henry Lovelich
notes: The poem's terminal prayer names the translator in the spelling 'Herry Lonelich'; modern scholarship and DIMEV normalize the name as Henry Lovelich.

agents.name: Frederick James Furnivall
notes: Editor of the four EETS parts carrying the poem and of the earlier two-volume Roxburghe Club edition.

assertion.notes: The authorial responsibility applies to the Middle English poem, not to its anonymous Old French source or Furnivall's editorial prose.
Lovelich translated and adapted the Vulgate Estoire del Saint Graal into Middle English rhyming couplets; his exact physical French exemplar remains unidentified.

scope.description: The 1874–1878 EETS source-edition representation of the poem

notes: Source-edition responsibility, not medieval intellectual authorship.

assertion.notes: This work date is kept separate from the date of the surviving physical witness.
The Parker catalog also supplies the broader designation fifteenth century; the narrower range is a manuscript date, not a composition date.

date_statements.display: First verified publication in Furnivall's two-volume Roxburghe Club edition, 1861–1863

scope.description: Earliest publication of the Middle English work verified in the inspected edition and scholarly bibliography

assertion.notes: The sources inspected identify Furnivall's 1861 and 1863 volumes as the earliest verified edition; 'first verified publication' avoids claiming that a still-unidentified earlier printing cannot exist. Later EETS publication dates remain in lineage only.

places.label: Essex
notes: LALME/eLALME linguistic profile 6360 localizes the unique witness language to Essex; this is not a claim that Lovelich composed the work there.

scope.description: Language of the unique surviving manuscript witness

assertion.notes: DIMEV links CamCCC80 to LALME/eLALME linguistic profile 6360 and grid 557181. Witness dialect is not silently promoted into place of composition.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: This is the language of Lovelich's work. The Old French and Modern English encoded passages are edition layers.

form_statements.description: A long Middle English narrative poem in rhyming couplets, predominantly four-stress lines. Furnivall's prose supplies, explanations, arguments, and notes do not make the abstract work mixed-form.

assertion.notes: The narrative joins Grail and Arthurian transmission to Joseph of Arimathea, conversion, Christian kingship, miracle, prophecy, and prayer.

summaries.value: Henry Lovelich's long Middle English rhyming-couplet translation of the Vulgate Estoire del Saint Graal recounts the Grail's early sacred history: Joseph of Arimathea and his followers, the conversion and adventures of Evalach and Seraphe, the Grail lineage, marvels and prophecies, and the transmission leading toward Arthurian Britain. The sole surviving manuscript begins imperfectly. CME00135's BODY follows Furnivall's four-part edition from the first surviving English line through Lovelich's closing prayer, with clearly scoped editorial French and Modern English interventions; its separate FRONT retains only a half-title from Dorothy Kempe's 1905 Part V study.

The encoded BODY has 46 numbered chapters, XI–LVI. It begins at Furnivall Part I p. 100 because the lost English opening was replaced in the edition by Old French on pp. 1–99, represented in the XML only by a GAP. The English poem continues through Part II, Part III, and Part IV p. 356. Furnivall inserts Old French on pp. 304–308 for a later physical lacuna, prints an Old French Turning Isle explanation on pp. 337–340 for material omitted by the translator, supplies one bracketed Modern English summary in chapter XXXVI, and appends a French explicit from Add MS 10292 in a TAILNOTE.

Work-level verse, Middle English language, authorship, translation, date, and place are modeled separately from manuscript production, manuscript dialect, editorial prose, source-edition carrier pages, and digital encoding. Furnivall's 1874–1878 textual Parts I–IV and Kempe's separately issued 1905 Part V study are distinct lineage entities. Royal MS 14 E III is Furnivall's documented French supply and comparison witness, not Lovelich's demonstrated exemplar. The displaced leaves in CCCC MS 80 are read in textual order as ff. 197r–198v followed by ff. 1r–88v.

extent.description: Cataloged at the single-work depth: one medieval verse work, with no prose-only or mixed intellectual items. Representation metrics remain separate: the XML has one FRONT half-title and 46 editorial BODY chapter divisions XI–LVI; chapters XXIV, XXV, and XXVII contain direct Old French editorial prose and chapter XXXVI contains a direct Modern English editorial summary. The XML's 49 LG containers and 23,784 L elements are retained as raw encoding metrics, not a reconciled manuscript line count: DIMEV reports 23,932 lines and another secondary count reports 23,922.

content_parts.label: Retained EETS series half-title
content_parts.summary: Three short FRONT paragraphs reading 'The Legend of the Holy Grail', 'EETS', and 'Extra Series No XCV 1905'. This is a source-carrier half-title from Kempe's Part V, not a chapter or title leaf of Lovelich's medieval work.

content_parts.label: Surviving History of the Holy Grail text
content_parts.summary: The single medieval verse work, surviving from its first extant English line through Lovelich's prayer. Furnivall's 46-chapter representation and explicitly modeled prose interventions are edition layers, not additional or mixed-form intellectual items.

content_parts.label: Old French Turning Isle explanation
content_parts.summary: An Old French account of Yl Torneawnt printed and encoded because Lovelich omitted the episode in translation. It is a source-text omission, not a demonstrated physical gap in the English manuscript.

content_parts.summary: A terminal Old French explicit from Add MS 10292 retained by CME00135 as a TAILNOTE after Lovelich's English conclusion.

related_works.title: Estoire del Saint Graal
notes: The source is the anonymous Old French prose Vulgate Estoire work. Neither Royal MS 14 E III nor Add MS 10292 is asserted as Lovelich's physical exemplar.

related_works.title: Merlin
notes: A separate Lovelich translation follows the Grail work in CCCC MS 80, beginning on f. 88v and continuing to f. 200v. Its shared codex and shared boundary leaf do not make it part of CME00135.

open_questions.question: Why do the XML's 23,784 L elements and DIMEV's 23,932 manuscript lines disagree, and how do both relate to the separate 23,922-verse count reported elsewhere?
next_steps: Collate the complete XML L sequence against Furnivall Parts I–IV and the Parker images.
Separate Middle English verse from the pp. 304–308 French lacuna supply and pp. 337–340 translator-omission explanation.
Document each source's counting convention before normalizing any work-level extent.

open_questions.question: Which physical Old French Estoire manuscript or textual branch did Lovelich use, and how should the poem's medieval Robert de Boron attribution be evaluated?
next_steps: Consult modern textual studies of Lovelich and the Vulgate Estoire.
Compare Lovelich's readings and omissions against multiple French witnesses.
Do not promote Royal MS 14 E III, Add MS 10292, or the Robert de Boron claim into direct-source or responsibility assertions without textual evidence.

open_questions.question: Can the 1420s London composition claim and the unique witness's Essex dialect localization be independently refined without conflating composition, copying, and linguistic localization?
next_steps: Consult the current scholarship behind the Database of Middle English Romance date and place fields.
Inspect the LALME/eLALME linguistic profile and its underlying features for CamCCC80.
Retain distinct work-composition, manuscript-date, and witness-dialect assertions.

notes: CME00135's FRONT is only the EETS Extra Series no. 95 half-title from Dorothy Kempe's separately issued 1905 Part V study. The medieval work is represented in BODY chapters XI–LVI from Furnivall's 1874–1878 textual Parts I–IV.

The lost English opening, Furnivall's Old French replacement opening, the later manuscript lacuna, and Lovelich's omission of the Turning Isle episode are four distinct textual facts and are not collapsed into one generic gap.
The unique manuscript's correct narrative order is ff. 197r–198v followed by ff. 1r–88v; present folio order alone is misleading.
Royal MS 14 E III and Add MS 10292 are Furnivall's documented French comparison or supply witnesses. Neither is asserted as the French manuscript physically used by Lovelich.
Work-level form is verse and primary language is Middle English. Encoded Old French and Modern English prose belongs to source-edition intervention and paratext layers.

The complete 414-canvas Parker facsimile has been cached and the work's exact canvas interval is recorded; unavailable British Library facsimiles remain requestable through the routes documented in the lineage manifest.
